outlook.headline: Memory supply stays tight, but capacity buildout is starting to catch up
outlook.summary: Memory production remains supply-constrained, but the next 1-2 quarters should show gradual improvement as Micron, TSMC, and related suppliers accelerate clean-room and fab expansion to meet strong demand and higher wafer starts. Tight DRAM/NAND conditions still support the equipment and materials chain, while Intel is the main loser from pricier constrained inputs; watch whether new capacity actually converts into output fast enough to ease bottlenecks.

drivers.label: Accelerated clean-room and fab buildouts
drivers.detail: Producers are speeding capacity additions to relieve bottlenecks and support more wafer starts.

drivers.label: Memory remains supply constrained
drivers.detail: DRAM and NAND commentary points to constrained output, keeping pricing and utilization elevated near term.

drivers.label: Rising utilization and wafer starts
drivers.detail: Higher wafer starts and utilization imply production is ramping, but from a tight starting point rather than an oversupplied one.

drivers.label: Disciplined bit growth/limited greenfield capacity
drivers.detail: Node-driven growth and limited new greenfield supply suggest the market stays disciplined instead of flooding.

risks: New clean-room space ramps faster than expected, easing tightness sooner and weakening the scarcity narrative.
Balanced reads from KLA, Amkor, and Ichor could imply more available capacity than the bullish supply-tight signals suggest.
If memory inputs stay expensive, demand-sensitive buyers could pull back and slow the need for incremental production.
